frame: Marital Rape: A Husband's Right, A Wife's Duty
generated_comment: With all due respect to everyone commenting here, I think we need to step back and look at this practically. Marriage in India is not just a contract between two individuals — it is a sacred institution involving two families, built on trust, compromise, and mutual duty. The concept of 'consent every time' is honestly a Western import that does not reflect how Indian marriages actually function. When a wife and husband share a home, share finances, raise children together, there is an implied understanding — this is how it has always been and it has held our families together for generations. My concern is very real: if this law passes, it will become the easiest weapon for wives who want to extort their husbands, settle property disputes, or simply walk out of a marriage with leverage. We already see misuse of Section 498A — every lawyer will tell you this. I am not saying a husband should be cruel or forceful, obviously that is wrong, but the law cannot enter the bedroom of a married couple. That is a private space. Hamare ghar ki baat court mein nahi jaani chahiye. The moment you criminalize the marital relationship like this, you are essentially telling Indian men that their own wives can destroy their lives with one complaint. Who will want to get married then? We are protecting marriage, not women's rights — because ultimately a stable marriage IS the best protection a woman can have.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: The comment encodes the entitlement frame — treating spousal sexual access as an implied marital duty and framing consent as a foreign imposition — while presenting itself as a reasonable defence of cultural tradition and marriage stability. It also deploys the 'false accusation' trope to pre-emptively delegitimise wives who might use such a law, a common surface manifestation of institutional/structural misogyny that positions women's legal recourse as inherently suspect.
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A man in Mumbai. You are commenting under your real name on a Facebook post where someone has shared an article about marital rape legislation. The comment section is divided. You are writing a detailed comment explaining why you think criminalising marital rape will destroy Indian marriages, why consent within marriage is a western concept that does not map onto Indian family structures, and what you think wives who use this law will use it for.
